title: A design systems role in accessibility
content:
How can a design system contribute to accessibility in a complex ecosystem of web apps?

SEB develops and maintains hundreds of web apps. Perhaps even thousands, if you count various internal tools. This is developed and mainteined by hundreds of developers across many, many teams.

“But wait a minute,” you may be thinking now. “I’ve only every used internetbanken, that’s one app??

Well, not quite. Internetbanken consists of many different parts. Pretty much every page is a separate app, and sometimes there are even multiple apps on one page. These apps are called micro-frontends, or MFEs for short. This is an agile architectural approach that enables software development at scale and makes it possible to distribute the work among many different teams.

This does leave a lot of room for variation however. Varying degrees of knowledge, different tastes. Some developers have eyes for visual design, for others it’s all about function. For some, a11y is front and center, for others, more of an afterthough.

This is all fine – we’re all different, and that’s ok. But when the goal is to deliver a cohesive and accessible customer experience, we need to find a common baseline. That’s the role of the design system!

## The tech stack

To understand a bit better where the design system fits into all of this, consider the front-end tech stack.

It can be roughly illustrated as a pyramid with three segments.

![](/assets/blog/a11y-mistakes-0.png)

At the base, we have the web platform itself, or more concretely, the browser.

Then, on the middle layer, there is typically some kind of front-end framework. At SEB, most apps use Angular or React.

And finally, at the top of the pyramid sits the app code. Business logic and UI code specific to this app. This is where the aforementioned variation comes in, both good and bad.

![](/assets/blog/a11y-mistakes-1.png)

And this is also where a11y mistakes happen. Missing semantics, unlabeled elements, or even bad use of ARIA, all happen in that top segment.

So this is where the design system enters the picture.

![](/assets/blog/a11y-mistakes-2.png)

By wedging itself between the framework and app code layers, the design system, with it's component library, can eliminate the need for individual app teams to write the nitty-gritty UI code where many of those a11y mistakes typically happen.

And of course, it also brings with it the visual cohesion for all those elements!

## Greens approach

So what actually goes into this design system layer?

At the core of the design system lies our component library, aptly named Green Core. This is a library of framework agnostic web components. Components are accessible by default and respects user preferences.

We also provide a component-based declarative layout system, paired with an accessible design token setup with accessible contrasts and built-in responsive features.

In addition to these component library features, we also provide extensive guidelines and documentation on our site at seb.io and in Storybook. We have a built-in MCP for coding agents, so they can consume this documentation and finally we provide bookable coaching sessions, where teams can get hands-on help from us in real-time.

## Components as encapsulated semantic entities

In Green Core, we think of each component as a discrete encapsulated semantic entity. That’s just a fancy way of saying that each component has design, behavior and accessibility built-in out of the box.

This encapsulation is sometimes as simple as an inner `<button>` element to provide button semantics and behavior, but can also be more involved with multiple related internal aria-roles and associated behavior.

For example, the Dropdown component includes the roles combobox, listbox, option, textbox and dialog, and various related aria-attributes and states. This is an example of a component which seems simple, but is actually quite tricky to get right, and there are multiple ways of doing it with various pros and cons.

When a developer uses `<gds-dropdown>`, they don’t need to deal with any of that, and instead they can just think of it as a single semantic element and focus on building business logic and customer experience instead.

## Accessibility over configuration

By making it harder to make certain mistakes, the likelihood of those mistakes is reduced.
In terms of atomic design, many of our components lean more towards molecules rather than simple atoms. This leaves less room for mistakes on the consumer side.

For example, GdsInput encapsulates label, text field, description text, expandable description text, invalid state and error message, all in one component. You just hand the different pieces of state and content to the component, and it takes care of rendering it correctly and semantically, using attributes like aria-describedby internally.

So here again, that complexity is lifted from the application developer, and they can just think of it as an input component, plain and simple.

## A11y challenges with web components

Web components allow us to deliver an accessible component library in a framework agnostic way. At SEB, this is an important consideration because we’re working with a heterogenic front-end tech stack. Each team is free to pick the stack that works best for them and their use case.

Another primary reason is shadow DOM. In a micro front-end arachitecture, where multiple separate application can co-exist on the same page, style encapsulation is paramount to prevent styling conflicts and prevent tricky version lock-in scenarios across teams.

It’s not all fun and games though. Web components, and in particular shadow DOM, has some relevant implications when it comes to accessibility.

The web platform is kind of like a mosaic of standards, rather than a neatly organized and homogenic system. Sometimes new features in the standard fails to account for how to interoperate with other features.

Aria association is one such case where, for a long time, there wasn’t really any way to associate elements between different document fragments, or shadow roots. This meant that, in cases such as the aforementioned GdsInput, if you had some external piece of text that would have made sense to associate with the input using aria-describedby, you wouldn’t be able to. Or more precisely, you couldn't associate it with the actual input element inside GdsInput.

In recent years though, this problem has been solved! There is now a standard available, called ARIAMixins. This allows us to set up these kinds of associations programmatically with DOM references in Javascript.

In practice, for Green Core, this has allowed us to provide an API on our components to handle this very similar to how you would normally do it with aria attributes. Namely, `gds-aria-*` attributes! For example, we now expose a `gds-aria-describedby` attribute on GdsInput, where you can supply a list of IDs to elements in your document (i.e, in the “light DOM”), which GdsInput will then resolve to DOM refs and assign internally via the ARIAMixins API.
